AMR Data Visualization in R

A Step-by-Step Teaching Guide for AMR Surveillance Professionals

Alfred Bortey (DR. AMR)

May 07, 2026

Welcome to AMR Data Visualization

By the end of this guide you will be able to:

  • Import real-world AMR data from Excel into R (including messy, unclean data)
  • Clean and prepare the data for analysis
  • Create five core chart types used in AMR surveillance
  • Interpret each chart in a public health context
  • Save publication-quality figures

Time needed: Approximately 3–4 hours (work through it section by section).

1 Before You Start

1.1 What you need installed

Run this once to install all packages used in this guide.
You only need to do this one time — after that, just load the packages with library().

# ── Run this block ONCE to install all required packages ──────────────────
# (Remove the # from the install.packages line below if needed)

# install.packages(c(
#   "readxl",      # read Excel files
#   "dplyr",       # data manipulation
#   "tidyr",       # reshaping data
#   "stringr",     # text/string cleaning
#   "lubridate",   # working with dates
#   "ggplot2",     # all charts
#   "scales",      # percentage labels on axes
#   "viridis",     # colourblind-friendly palettes
#   "patchwork",   # combine multiple plots
#   "janitor",     # clean column names
#   "knitr",       # table formatting in reports
#   "kableExtra"   # extra table styling
# ))

1.2 Load packages

Every time you open R and start working, you need to load the packages:

library(readxl)      # reading Excel files
library(dplyr)       # data manipulation: filter, mutate, group_by, summarise
library(tidyr)       # reshaping: pivot_longer, pivot_wider
library(stringr)     # string cleaning: str_to_upper, str_trim, str_detect
library(lubridate)   # date parsing: dmy(), mdy(), ymd()
library(ggplot2)     # all visualizations
library(scales)      # axis label formatting (%, commas)
library(viridis)     # colour palettes accessible to colourblind readers
library(patchwork)   # combine multiple ggplot2 charts side by side
library(janitor)     # clean_names() standardises column names
library(knitr)       # kable() for neat tables
library(kableExtra)  # extra kable formatting

1.3 Set a consistent plot theme

We define one theme here and reuse it on every chart so they all look consistent. Think of this like setting a style template in PowerPoint — once, not on every slide.

# ── Consistent chart theme ────────────────────────────────────────────────
amr_theme <- theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(colour = "#1A4A8A", face = "bold",
                                 size = 14, hjust = 0),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "#7f8c8d", size = 11, hjust = 0),
    plot.caption  = element_text(colour = "#aaaaaa", size = 9,
                                 hjust = 1, face = "italic"),
    axis.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    axis.text     = element_text(size = 10),
    legend.position  = "bottom",
    legend.title     = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#1A4A8A")
  )

# Apply this theme globally — all charts will use it automatically
theme_set(amr_theme)

2 Importing Data from Excel

Why this step matters

In real AMR surveillance, data rarely arrives in a perfectly clean format. It typically comes from:

  • A WHONET export (.csv or .dbf)
  • A lab information system (LIS) export (.xlsx)
  • A paper form that someone typed into Excel
  • A DHIMS2 or HMIS download

Our teaching Excel file (AMR_Teaching_Data.xlsx) has been designed to look like real lab data — with inconsistent names, mixed date formats, blank rows, and extra columns. We will import it and clean it together.

2.1 Looking at the Excel workbook first

Our workbook has five sheets. Let’s see what is available:

# ── Path to your Excel file ───────────────────────────────────────────────
# IMPORTANT: Change this path to wherever you saved the Excel file.
# Examples:
#   Windows:  "C:/Users/YourName/Documents/AMR_Teaching_Data.xlsx"
#   Mac/Linux: "/home/yourname/AMR_Teaching_Data.xlsx"

excel_file <- "AMR_Teaching_Data.xlsx"

# List all sheets in the workbook
excel_sheets(excel_file)
## [1] "README"       "Raw_AST_Data" "Consumption"  "MIC_Values"   "Quarterly_R"

The output shows all available sheets. We will use:

Sheet What it contains
Raw_AST_Data 130 isolate records — messy, realistic
Consumption Monthly antibiotic use (DDD & DOT)
MIC_Values Raw MIC values for histogram and box plot
Quarterly_R Pre-aggregated quarterly resistance rates

2.2 Import Sheet 1: Raw AST data (the messy sheet)

# ── Import the raw AST data ───────────────────────────────────────────────
# skip = 0 means start from row 1 (the header row)
# .name_repair = "unique" handles any duplicate column names

raw_ast <- read_excel(
  excel_file,
  sheet = "Raw_AST_Data",
  skip  = 0,
  .name_repair = "unique"
)

# Quick look at the data
cat("Rows:", nrow(raw_ast), "\n")
## Rows: 134
cat("Columns:", ncol(raw_ast), "\n\n")
## Columns: 17
# Show first 8 rows
head(raw_ast, 8)

What you will notice in this data (and why it is realistic)

  • Organism names are inconsistent: "Klebsiella pneumoniae", "K. pneumoniae", "KLEBSIELLA PNEUMONIAE" all appear
  • Some AST results say "R", others say "r" or "res" — same meaning, different format
  • Dates are in mixed formats: 2022-01-05, 05/01/2022, Jan 05 2022
  • There are blank rows (common in Excel exports)
  • There is a row that is a repeated header (another common Excel artifact)
  • Columns like EXTRA_COL_IGNORE and Notes are irrelevant

This is normal. The next section shows you how to fix all of this.

3 Data Cleaning: Making the Data Ready for Analysis

Cleaning data is not glamorous, but it is the most important step. Bad data produces misleading results. This section walks through each cleaning step and explains why each one is necessary.

3.1 Step 1 — Remove completely blank rows

Blank rows cause errors later when R tries to count isolates or calculate percentages.

# ── Remove rows where ALL key columns are empty ───────────────────────────
raw_ast <- raw_ast %>%
  # filter() keeps rows where the condition is TRUE
  # !is.na() means "is NOT missing"
  # We check PatientID — if it is missing, the row is blank or a repeat header
  filter(!is.na(PatientID) & PatientID != "PatientID")
  # PatientID != "PatientID" removes any rows where the header was repeated

cat("Rows remaining after removing blanks:", nrow(raw_ast), "\n")
## Rows remaining after removing blanks: 130

3.2 Step 2 — Standardise column names

Column names with spaces, capital letters or special characters cause problems in R code. janitor::clean_names() converts everything to lowercase with underscores:

raw_ast <- raw_ast %>%
  clean_names()

# See the new column names
names(raw_ast)
##  [1] "patient_id"       "ward"             "collection_date"  "specimen_type"   
##  [5] "organism"         "amp"              "amc"              "ctx"             
##  [9] "mem"              "cip"              "gen"              "sxt"             
## [13] "van"              "oxa"              "col"              "extra_col_ignore"
## [17] "notes"
head(raw_ast)

Notice how CollectionDate became collection_date, PatientID became patient_id, etc. This makes typing easier and prevents errors.

3.3 Step 3 — Remove irrelevant columns

# Drop columns we do not need
raw_ast <- raw_ast %>%
  dplyr::select(-extra_col_ignore, -notes)
  # select(-colname) removes a column

3.4 Step 4 — Standardise organism names

This is one of the most important steps in AMR surveillance data cleaning. We group all spelling variants into one standard name.

# ── Standardise organism names ────────────────────────────────────────────
# str_detect() searches for a pattern inside a text string
# str_to_upper() converts text to ALL CAPS so we can match regardless of case

raw_ast <- raw_ast %>%
  mutate(
    # First, convert everything to uppercase for matching
    org_upper = str_to_upper(str_trim(organism)),

    # Then assign a standard name based on what we detect
    organism_std = case_when(
      str_detect(org_upper, "KLEBSIELLA|K\\. PNEUM")   ~ "Klebsiella pneumoniae",
      str_detect(org_upper, "ESCHERICHIA|E\\. COLI|E\\.COLI") ~ "Escherichia coli",
      str_detect(org_upper, "STAPHYLOCOCCUS|STAPH|MRSA|S\\. AUREUS") ~ "Staphylococcus aureus",
      str_detect(org_upper, "PSEUDOMONAS|P\\. AERUG") ~ "Pseudomonas aeruginosa",
      str_detect(org_upper, "ACINETOBACTER|A\\. BAUM") ~ "Acinetobacter baumannii",
      TRUE ~ organism   # keep original if nothing matched (so we can review)
    )
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(-org_upper)  # remove the temporary column

# Check: how many unique organism names do we have now?
cat("Unique organism names after standardisation:\n")
## Unique organism names after standardisation:
print(table(raw_ast$organism_std))
## 
## Acinetobacter baumannii        Escherichia coli   Klebsiella pneumoniae 
##                      17                      43                      33 
##  Pseudomonas aeruginosa                S.aureus   Staphylococcus aureus 
##                      11                       4                      22

3.5 Step 5 — Standardise AST results (R/I/S)

# ── Standardise AST result notation ──────────────────────────────────────
# All columns from 'amp' to 'col' contain AST results
# We want only R, I, S, or NA

ast_cols <- c("amp","amc","ctx","mem","cip","gen","sxt","van","oxa","col")

standardise_ris <- function(x) {
  # Convert to uppercase and trim whitespace first
  x <- str_to_upper(str_trim(as.character(x)))
  case_when(
    x %in% c("R", "RES", "RESISTANT")       ~ "R",
    x %in% c("I", "INT", "INTERMEDIATE")    ~ "I",
    x %in% c("S", "SUS", "SUSCEPTIBLE")     ~ "S",
    x %in% c("NA", "N/A", "", "NULL")       ~ NA_character_,
    TRUE                                     ~ NA_character_  # anything else → NA
  )
}

raw_ast <- raw_ast %>%
  mutate(across(
    all_of(ast_cols[ast_cols %in% names(raw_ast)]),
    standardise_ris
  ))

# Verify: should only see R, I, S, or NA now
cat("Unique CIP values after standardisation:\n")
## Unique CIP values after standardisation:
print(table(raw_ast$cip, useNA = "ifany"))
## 
##  I  R  S 
## 29 53 48

3.6 Step 6 — Parse dates

Mixed date formats are extremely common in lab data across Africa and LMICs. lubridate can handle multiple formats with parse_date_time():

# ── Parse dates from multiple formats ────────────────────────────────────
# parse_date_time() tries each format in order until one works
raw_ast <- raw_ast %>%
  mutate(
    collection_date = parse_date_time(
      collection_date,
      orders = c("ymd", "dmy", "mdy", "d b Y", "d-b-Y"),
      quiet  = TRUE  # suppress warnings for each try
    ),
    # Create time period columns for trend analysis
    year    = year(collection_date),
    quarter = paste0("Q", quarter(collection_date), "-", year(collection_date)),
    month   = month(collection_date, label = TRUE)
  )

cat("Date range in dataset:\n")
## Date range in dataset:
cat("Earliest:", as.character(min(raw_ast$collection_date, na.rm=TRUE)), "\n")
## Earliest: 2022-01-01
cat("Latest:  ", as.character(max(raw_ast$collection_date, na.rm=TRUE)), "\n")
## Latest:   2022-11-20

3.7 Step 7 — Apply the First Isolate Rule

What is the First Isolate Rule?

When the same patient has the same organism cultured multiple times (e.g., an ICU patient with a persistent K. pneumoniae bloodstream infection cultured every week), including all of those results in resistance calculations inflates the resistance rate.

The CLSI M39-A4 guideline requires that for cumulative susceptibility reporting (antibiograms), you keep only the first isolate per patient per organism within each analysis period.

# ── Apply First Isolate Rule ──────────────────────────────────────────────
cat("Records BEFORE First Isolate Rule:", nrow(raw_ast), "\n")
## Records BEFORE First Isolate Rule: 130
clean_data <- raw_ast %>%
  arrange(patient_id, organism_std, specimen_type, collection_date) %>%
  group_by(patient_id, organism_std) %>%
  mutate(
    isolate_rank  = row_number(),    # 1 = first isolate, 2 = second, etc.
    is_first      = (isolate_rank == 1)
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  filter(is_first) %>%              # keep only first isolates
  dplyr::select(-isolate_rank, -is_first)

cat("Records AFTER First Isolate Rule:", nrow(clean_data), "\n")
## Records AFTER First Isolate Rule: 128
cat("Isolates excluded:", nrow(raw_ast) - nrow(clean_data), "\n")
## Isolates excluded: 2

4 Diiferent code for first isolate rule

This considers situations where you might have genuinely different isolates from a patient because they have multiple infections from different sites or sources.

# ── Apply First Isolate Rule ──────────────────────────────────────────────
cat("Records BEFORE First Isolate Rule:", nrow(raw_ast), "\n")
## Records BEFORE First Isolate Rule: 130
clean_data <- raw_ast %>%
  arrange(patient_id, organism_std, specimen_type, collection_date) %>%
  group_by(patient_id, organism_std, specimen_type) %>%
  mutate(
    isolate_rank  = row_number(),    # 1 = first isolate, 2 = second, etc.
    is_first      = (isolate_rank == 1)
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  filter(is_first) %>%              # keep only first isolates
  dplyr::select(-isolate_rank, -is_first)

cat("Records AFTER First Isolate Rule:", nrow(clean_data), "\n")
## Records AFTER First Isolate Rule: 130
cat("Isolates excluded:", nrow(raw_ast) - nrow(clean_data), "\n")
## Isolates excluded: 0

4.1 Summary of cleaned data

Let’s check what we have:

# Organism frequency table
organism_summary <- clean_data %>%
  count(organism_std, name = "n_isolates") %>%
  mutate(percent = round(n_isolates / sum(n_isolates) * 100, 1)) %>%
  arrange(desc(n_isolates))

kable(organism_summary,
      col.names = c("Organism", "N isolates", "%"),
      caption   = "Table 1. Organism frequency after cleaning and deduplication") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE)
Table 1. Organism frequency after cleaning and deduplication
Organism N isolates %
Escherichia coli 43 33.1
Klebsiella pneumoniae 33 25.4
Staphylococcus aureus 22 16.9
Acinetobacter baumannii 17 13.1
Pseudomonas aeruginosa 11 8.5
S.aureus 4 3.1

5 Visualization 1: Bar Plot — Comparing Resistance Rates

When to use a bar plot in AMR surveillance

Bar plots are used when you want to compare a single number (like % resistant) across different categories (like organisms, wards, or antibiotics).

They are the most common chart in AMR reports because they are easy to read and communicate clearly to clinical and non-technical audiences.

AMR examples: - Ciprofloxacin resistance in K. pneumoniae vs E. coli vs P. aeruginosa - MRSA rates across different wards - Resistance to different antibiotics for the same organism

5.1 Calculate resistance rates

Before plotting, we need to calculate what we want to show. We will calculate % resistant with a 95% confidence interval (CI).

# ── Calculate resistance rates with 95% Wilson Score CI ──────────────────
# Why Wilson Score CI? It works correctly even when n is small or
# the proportion is near 0% or 100% — both very common in AMR data.

calc_resistance_ci <- function(data, organism_name, antibiotic) {
  col <- antibiotic   # the column in our data

  data %>%
    filter(organism_std == organism_name, !is.na(.data[[col]])) %>%
    summarise(
      organism   = organism_name,
      antibiotic = toupper(antibiotic),
      n_tested   = n(),
      n_R        = sum(.data[[col]] == "R", na.rm = TRUE),
      .groups    = "drop"
    ) %>%
    mutate(
      pct_R = round(n_R / n_tested * 100, 1),
      # Wilson Score CI calculation
      p      = n_R / n_tested,
      z      = 1.96,
      denom  = 1 + z^2 / n_tested,
      centre = (p + z^2 / (2 * n_tested)) / denom,
      margin = z * sqrt(p*(1-p)/n_tested + z^2/(4*n_tested^2)) / denom,
      ci_lo  = round(pmax(0, (centre - margin)*100), 1),
      ci_hi  = round(pmin(100, (centre + margin)*100), 1)
    ) %>%
    dplyr::select(organism, antibiotic, n_tested, n_R, pct_R, ci_lo, ci_hi)
}

# Calculate for ciprofloxacin across all priority organisms
cip_resistance <- bind_rows(
  calc_resistance_ci(clean_data, "Klebsiella pneumoniae",  "cip"),
  calc_resistance_ci(clean_data, "Escherichia coli",       "cip"),
  calc_resistance_ci(clean_data, "Staphylococcus aureus",  "cip"),
  calc_resistance_ci(clean_data, "Pseudomonas aeruginosa", "cip"),
  calc_resistance_ci(clean_data, "Acinetobacter baumannii","cip")
)

# Show the table
kable(cip_resistance,
      col.names = c("Organism","Antibiotic","N tested","N resistant",
                    "% Resistant","CI lower","CI upper"),
      caption   = "Table 2. Ciprofloxacin resistance rates with 95% Wilson Score CI") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"))
Table 2. Ciprofloxacin resistance rates with 95% Wilson Score CI
Organism Antibiotic N tested N resistant % Resistant CI lower CI upper
Klebsiella pneumoniae CIP 33 12 36.4 22.2 53.4
Escherichia coli CIP 43 18 41.9 28.4 56.7
Staphylococcus aureus CIP 22 7 31.8 16.4 52.7
Pseudomonas aeruginosa CIP 11 6 54.5 28.0 78.7
Acinetobacter baumannii CIP 17 9 52.9 31.0 73.8

#STREAMLINED CALCULATION Without having specify ncalculation for each antibiotic like in the previous section.

# ── Calculate resistance rates with 95% Wilson Score CI ──────────────────
# Why Wilson Score CI? It works correctly even when n is small or
# the proportion is near 0% or 100% — both very common in AMR data.

calc_resistance_ci <- function(data, organism_name, antibiotic) {
  col <- antibiotic   # the column in our data

  data %>%
    filter(organism_std == organism_name, !is.na(.data[[col]])) %>%
    summarise(
      organism   = organism_name,
      antibiotic = toupper(antibiotic),
      n_tested   = n(),
      n_R        = sum(.data[[col]] == "R", na.rm = TRUE),
      .groups    = "drop"
    ) %>%
    mutate(
      pct_R = round(n_R / n_tested * 100, 1),
      # Wilson Score CI calculation
      p      = n_R / n_tested,
      z      = 1.96,
      denom  = 1 + z^2 / n_tested,
      centre = (p + z^2 / (2 * n_tested)) / denom,
      margin = z * sqrt(p*(1-p)/n_tested + z^2/(4*n_tested^2)) / denom,
      ci_lo  = round(pmax(0, (centre - margin)*100), 1),
      ci_hi  = round(pmin(100, (centre + margin)*100), 1)
    ) %>%
    dplyr::select(organism, antibiotic, n_tested, n_R, pct_R, ci_lo, ci_hi)
}

#Select antibiotic columns
antibiotics <- names(clean_data)[
  names(clean_data) %in% c("cip","gen","amk","ctx","fox","mem")
]
# Priority organisms
priority_organisms <- c(
  "Klebsiella pneumoniae",
  "Escherichia coli",
  "Staphylococcus aureus",
  "Pseudomonas aeruginosa",
  "Acinetobacter baumannii"
)

# Create combinations
all_combinations <- expand.grid(
  organism = priority_organisms,
  antibiotic = antibiotics,
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

# Calculate resistance
all_resistance <- all_combinations %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  do(
    calc_resistance_ci(
      clean_data,
      .$organism,
      .$antibiotic
    )
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

# Show the table
kable(all_resistance,
      col.names = c("Organism","Antibiotic","N tested","N resistant",
                    "% Resistant","CI lower","CI upper"),
      caption = "Table 2. Resistance rates with 95% Wilson CI") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"))
Table 2. Resistance rates with 95% Wilson CI
Organism Antibiotic N tested N resistant % Resistant CI lower CI upper
Klebsiella pneumoniae CTX 33 14 42.4 27.2 59.2
Escherichia coli CTX 43 13 30.2 18.6 45.1
Staphylococcus aureus CTX 0 0 NaN NaN NaN
Pseudomonas aeruginosa CTX 11 5 45.5 21.3 72.0
Acinetobacter baumannii CTX 0 0 NaN NaN NaN
Klebsiella pneumoniae MEM 33 11 33.3 19.8 50.4
Escherichia coli MEM 43 6 14.0 6.6 27.3
Staphylococcus aureus MEM 0 0 NaN NaN NaN
Pseudomonas aeruginosa MEM 11 3 27.3 9.7 56.6
Acinetobacter baumannii MEM 17 9 52.9 31.0 73.8
Klebsiella pneumoniae CIP 33 12 36.4 22.2 53.4
Escherichia coli CIP 43 18 41.9 28.4 56.7
Staphylococcus aureus CIP 22 7 31.8 16.4 52.7
Pseudomonas aeruginosa CIP 11 6 54.5 28.0 78.7
Acinetobacter baumannii CIP 17 9 52.9 31.0 73.8
Klebsiella pneumoniae GEN 33 12 36.4 22.2 53.4
Escherichia coli GEN 43 11 25.6 14.9 40.2
Staphylococcus aureus GEN 22 8 36.4 19.7 57.0
Pseudomonas aeruginosa GEN 11 5 45.5 21.3 72.0
Acinetobacter baumannii GEN 17 11 64.7 41.3 82.7

Reading the confidence interval

The 95% CI tells you the range within which the true resistance rate probably lies. A wide CI (e.g., 20–60%) means you have too few isolates tested — interpret with caution. A narrow CI (e.g., 44–52%) means you have enough data to be confident in the estimate.

Rule of thumb: Only report a resistance rate formally if n ≥ 30 isolates.

5.2 Create the bar plot

# ── Add a colour variable based on resistance level ───────────────────────
cip_resistance <- cip_resistance %>%
  mutate(
    res_category = case_when(
      pct_R >= 70 ~ "High (≥70%)",
      pct_R >= 40 ~ "Moderate (40–69%)",
      TRUE        ~ "Low (<40%)"
    ),
    # Short organism labels for the axis
    org_short = case_when(
      organism == "Klebsiella pneumoniae"   ~ "K. pneumoniae",
      organism == "Escherichia coli"        ~ "E. coli",
      organism == "Staphylococcus aureus"   ~ "S. aureus",
      organism == "Pseudomonas aeruginosa"  ~ "P. aeruginosa",
      organism == "Acinetobacter baumannii" ~ "A. baumannii",
      TRUE ~ organism
    )
  )

# ── Build the bar chart ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
p_bar <- ggplot(
  cip_resistance,
  aes(x    = reorder(org_short, -pct_R),  # reorder: tallest bar first
      y    = pct_R,
      fill = res_category)
) +
  # The bars
  geom_col(width = 0.6, colour = "white") +

  # Error bars showing the 95% CI
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = ci_lo, ymax = ci_hi),
    width = 0.2, colour = "grey30", linewidth = 0.7
  ) +

  # Labels above each bar: percentage and n
  geom_text(
    aes(label = paste0(pct_R, "%\n(n=", n_tested, ")")),
    vjust = -0.5, size = 3.2, colour = "grey20"
  ) +

  # Reference line at 50% — standard threshold for "high" resistance
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = 50, linetype = "dashed",
    colour = "#c0392b", linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text", x = 5.4, y = 52,
    label = "50% threshold", hjust = 1, size = 3, colour = "#c0392b"
  ) +

  # Colour fill: red for high, amber for moderate, green for low
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("High (≥70%)"       = "#e74c3c",
               "Moderate (40–69%)" = "#e99e22",
               "Low (<40%)"        = "#37ae69"),
    name = "Resistance level"
  ) +

  # y-axis: 0–110% to leave room for labels above bars
  scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(0, 110),
    labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%")
  ) +

  # Axis labels and title
  labs(
    title    = "Ciprofloxacin resistance by organism — 2022",
    subtitle = "First isolates only | Error bars = 95% Wilson Score CI | Dashed line = 50% threshold",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Resistance rate (%)",
    caption  = "Data source: Teaching dataset | EUCAST 2025 breakpoints"
  ) +

  # Apply our consistent theme
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(face = "italic", size = 11)
  )

# Display the plot
p_bar

# ── Save the plot as a high-quality PNG ──────────────────────────────────
# width and height are in inches; dpi = dots per inch (300 = print quality)
ggsave(
  filename = "bar_cip_resistance.png",
  plot     = p_bar,
  width    = 9,
  height   = 5.5,
  dpi      = 300
)

Interpreting this chart

Looking at the bar chart:

  • A. baumannii and P. aeruginosa show very high ciprofloxacin resistance (red bars). This means standard fluoroquinolones cannot be used empirically for infections caused by these organisms at this hospital.
  • S. aureus is in the moderate range — ciprofloxacin remains somewhat useful but should be confirmed by culture sensitivity.
  • E. coli and K. pneumoniae also show concerning levels of fluoroquinolone resistance.

Action implication: The infection control team should review fluoroquinolone prescribing and consider restricting empirical use for Gram-negative hospital-acquired infections.

Exercise 1

Modify the bar chart code above to show meropenem resistance instead of ciprofloxacin.

What do you notice about the meropenem resistance rates compared to ciprofloxacin? Why is this clinically important?

6 Visualization 2: Histogram — MIC Distributions

When to use a histogram in AMR surveillance

A histogram shows you the distribution (spread and shape) of a set of numbers. In AMR work, we use histograms for MIC (minimum inhibitory concentration) values to understand how a population of bacteria responds to an antibiotic.

Why this matters: - A unimodal (single-peak) distribution near the susceptible range is reassuring - A bimodal (two-peak) distribution suggests a resistant subpopulation is emerging - The position of the EUCAST/CLSI breakpoint relative to the peaks tells you how much of the population is captured on each side

6.1 Import MIC data

# ── Import MIC values from the Excel workbook ─────────────────────────────
mic_data <- read_excel(excel_file, sheet = "MIC_Values")

# Standardise column names
mic_data <- mic_data %>% clean_names()

# Preview
head(mic_data, 6)

6.2 Prepare MIC data for plotting

MIC values span a very wide range (0.03 to 64 mg/L or more). We use a log₂ scale because each doubling of MIC is one step in the standard serial dilution — so log₂ spacing is natural and standard in clinical microbiology.

# ── Focus on K. pneumoniae vs meropenem ──────────────────────────────────
kp_mem_mic <- mic_data %>%
  filter(
    organism == "K. pneumoniae",
    antibiotic == "Meropenem"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    # Convert MIC to log2 scale
    mic_log2 = log2(mic_mg_l),
    # Clean up the interpretation column
    interpretation = str_to_upper(str_trim(interpretation)),
    interpretation = factor(interpretation, levels = c("S","I","R"))
  )

cat("Number of K. pneumoniae meropenem isolates:", nrow(kp_mem_mic), "\n")
## Number of K. pneumoniae meropenem isolates: 32
cat("\nMIC value range:\n")
## 
## MIC value range:
print(summary(kp_mem_mic$mic_mgl))
## Warning: Unknown or uninitialised column: `mic_mgl`.
## Length  Class   Mode 
##      0   NULL   NULL

6.3 Create the histogram

# ── EUCAST 2025 breakpoint for K. pneumoniae meropenem ───────────────────
# Susceptible: MIC ≤ 2 mg/L → log2(2) = 1
bp_log2 <- log2(2)

# ── Create x-axis labels (convert log2 back to mg/L) ─────────────────────
# Standard MIC steps in clinical microbiology
mic_steps    <- c(0.03, 0.06, 0.12, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64)
log2_steps   <- log2(mic_steps)
mic_labels   <- c("0.03","0.06","0.12","0.25","0.5","1","2","4","8","16","32","64")

# ── Build the histogram ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
p_hist <- ggplot(
  kp_mem_mic,
  aes(x = mic_log2, fill = interpretation)
) +

  # Histogram bars — binwidth = 1 means one bar per doubling dilution
  geom_histogram(
    binwidth = 1,
    colour   = "white",
    boundary = 0.5     # align bins to the standard MIC steps
  ) +

  # Breakpoint line
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = bp_log2 + 0.5,   # +0.5 puts line at the boundary between S and R
    linetype   = "dashed",
    colour     = "#2c3e50",
    linewidth  = 1.2
  ) +

  # Annotation explaining the breakpoint line
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = bp_log2 + 0.7, y = Inf,
    label  = "EUCAST breakpoint\n(S ≤2 mg/L, R >2 mg/L)",
    hjust  = 0, vjust = 1.5,
    size   = 3.5, colour = "#2c3e50"
  ) +

  # Shaded background to show S and R zones
  annotate("rect", xmin = -Inf, xmax = bp_log2 + 0.5,
           ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf,
           fill = "#27ae60", alpha = 0.04) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = bp_log2 + 0.5, xmax = Inf,
           ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf,
           fill = "#e74c3c", alpha = 0.04) +

  # Colour fill for S/I/R
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("S" = "#27ae60", "I" = "#e67e22", "R" = "#e74c3c"),
    name   = "Interpretation",
    labels = c("S" = "Susceptible (S)",
               "I" = "Intermediate (I)",
               "R" = "Resistant (R)")
  ) +

  # x-axis: show real MIC values, not log2 numbers
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = log2_steps,
    labels = mic_labels
  ) +

  labs(
    title    = "K. pneumoniae meropenem MIC distribution",
    subtitle = "Each bar = one standard doubling dilution | Dashed line = EUCAST 2025 breakpoint",
    x        = "MIC (mg/L) — log₂ scale",
    y        = "Number of isolates",
    caption  = "Data source: Teaching dataset | n = 40 isolates"
  )

p_hist

Interpreting this histogram

Look at the shape of the distribution:

  • Susceptible cluster (left of the breakpoint): Most isolates have MICs of 0.06–1 mg/L, far below the EUCAST breakpoint of 2 mg/L. This suggests wild-type susceptibility.

  • Resistant cluster (right of the breakpoint): A smaller group of isolates has MICs of 8–32 mg/L. This bimodal (two-peak) shape is a warning sign — it means a resistant subpopulation is present alongside the susceptible wild type.

  • Clinical implication: Even though overall carbapenem resistance is moderate (~30%), the presence of a resistant cluster should trigger: (1) molecular testing for carbapenemase production (KPC, NDM, OXA), (2) strict contact precautions for carbapenem-resistant isolates, and (3) antibiotic stewardship review.

Exercise 2

The MIC_Values sheet also contains data for:

  • E. coli vs Ciprofloxacin
  • A. baumannii vs Meropenem

Repeat the histogram for one of these combinations.

Do you see a bimodal or unimodal distribution? What does the shape tell you?

7 Visualization 3: Box Plot — Comparing Distributions Across Groups

When to use a box plot in AMR surveillance

Box plots are useful when you want to compare the spread and central tendency of MIC values across multiple groups at once — such as comparing MIC distributions across different wards, different time periods, or different specimen types.

A single histogram shows you one group. A box plot shows you many groups side by side.

Reading a box plot:

  • The line inside the box = median (middle value)
  • The box edges = Q1 (25th percentile) and Q3 (75th percentile)
  • The whiskers extend to 1.5× the interquartile range
  • Dots beyond the whiskers = outliers (unusually high or low values)

7.1 Prepare data: MIC by ward

# ── Filter to K. pneumoniae ciprofloxacin for box plot ───────────────────
kp_cip_mic <- mic_data %>%
  filter(
    organism   == "K. pneumoniae",
    antibiotic == "Ciprofloxacin"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    mic_log2 = log2(mic_mg_l),
    ward     = factor(ward,
                      levels = c("ICU","SUR","MED","PED","OBS"))
  )

# How many isolates per ward?
kp_cip_mic %>%
  count(ward, name = "n") %>%
  kable(caption = "Table 3. K. pneumoniae isolates by ward") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "striped", full_width = FALSE)
Table 3. K. pneumoniae isolates by ward
ward n
ICU 8
SUR 10
MED 9
OBS 5

7.2 Create the box plot

# EUCAST CIP breakpoint for K. pneumoniae: S ≤1, R >1
bp_cip_log2 <- log2(1)

p_box <- ggplot(
  kp_cip_mic,
  aes(x    = ward,
      y    = mic_log2,
      fill = ward)
) +

  # The box plots
  geom_boxplot(
    width         = 0.5,
    colour        = "grey30",
    outlier.shape = 21,          # outlier points as open circles
    outlier.size  = 2.5,
    outlier.fill  = "white",
    outlier.colour= "grey40"
  ) +

  # Overlay individual data points (jittered so they don't overlap)
  geom_jitter(
    width  = 0.05,
    alpha  = 0.5,
    size   = 1.8,
    colour = "grey20"
  ) +

  # Breakpoint reference line
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = bp_cip_log2,
    linetype   = "dashed",
    colour     = "#c0392b",
    linewidth  = 0.9
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text", x = 5.6, y = bp_cip_log2 + 0.15,
    label = "EUCAST\nbreakpoint\n(1 mg/L)",
    hjust = 1, size = 3, colour = "#c0392b"
  ) +

  # Ward colours: one colour per ward
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("ICU" = "#3498db", "SUR" = "#e67e22",
               "MED" = "#27ae60", "PED" = "#9b59b6",
               "OBS" = "#e74c3c"),
    guide  = "none"   # legend not needed — wards labelled on x-axis
  ) +

  # y-axis: show real MIC values
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = log2(c(0.06, 0.12, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32)),
    labels = c("0.06","0.12","0.25","0.5","1","2","4","8","16","32")
  ) +

  labs(
    title    = "K. pneumoniae ciprofloxacin MIC distribution by ward",
    subtitle = "Each point = one isolate | Dashed line = EUCAST 2025 breakpoint (1 mg/L)",
    x        = "Ward",
    y        = "Ciprofloxacin MIC (mg/L) — log₂ scale",
    caption  = "Data source: Teaching dataset | First isolates only"
  )

p_box

Interpreting this box plot

  • MED has the highest median MIC (box sits highest on the y-axis), meaning ICU isolates tend to be more resistant. This is expected — ICU patients receive more antibiotic courses, creating selection pressure.
  • Obstetrics has the lowest median MIC — most isolates are susceptible.
  • Any ward where the median line is above the dashed breakpoint has >50% of its isolates classified as resistant. This is a red flag.
  • Outlier dots far above the main box indicate individual isolates with very high MICs — these could represent carbapenem-resistant or XDR strains worth investigating.

Action: Wards with high median MICs should have targeted stewardship reviews and tighter infection control measures.

8 Visualization 4: Scatter Plot — Trends Over Time and Correlations

Scatter plots in AMR surveillance are used for two distinct purposes:

  1. Trend over time — showing how resistance % changes across quarterly periods
  2. Correlation — showing whether antibiotic consumption is associated with rising resistance

8.1 4.1 Import trend and consumption data

# ── Import pre-aggregated quarterly resistance data ───────────────────────
quarterly_r <- read_excel(excel_file, sheet = "Quarterly_R") %>%
  clean_names()

# ── Import consumption data ───────────────────────────────────────────────
consumption <- read_excel(excel_file, sheet = "Consumption") %>%
  clean_names()

head(quarterly_r)

8.2 4.2 Trend scatter plot: Resistance over time

# ── Prepare the trend data ────────────────────────────────────────────────
# We will plot K. pneumoniae ciprofloxacin resistance (kp_cip) over time

kp_trend <- quarterly_r %>%
  dplyr:: select(quarter, time_id, pct_r = kp_cip) %>%
  mutate(
    quarter = factor(quarter, levels = c("Q1-2022","Q2-2022",
                                         "Q3-2022","Q4-2022"))
  )

# ── Fit a linear regression model ────────────────────────────────────────
model <- lm(pct_r ~ time_id, data = kp_trend)
summary(model)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = pct_r ~ time_id, data = kp_trend)
## 
## Residuals:
##     1     2     3     4 
##  0.33 -0.34 -0.31  0.32 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  68.9000     0.5632   122.3 6.68e-05 ***
## time_id       3.2700     0.2057    15.9  0.00393 ** 
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.4599 on 2 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.9922, Adjusted R-squared:  0.9882 
## F-statistic: 252.8 on 1 and 2 DF,  p-value: 0.003933
# Extract key statistics for the annotation
slope   <- round(coef(model)[2], 2)
r2      <- round(summary(model)$r.squared, 3)
p_value <- round(coef(summary(model))[2, 4], 4)

# Create prediction data for the regression line
pred_df <- data.frame(time_id = seq(1, 4, length.out = 50))
preds   <- predict(model, newdata = pred_df, interval = "confidence")
pred_df <- cbind(pred_df, preds)

# ── Build the scatter plot ────────────────────────────────────────────────
p_scatter <- ggplot(kp_trend, aes(x = time_id, y = pct_r)) +

  # Confidence interval ribbon (shaded area around regression line)
  geom_ribbon(
    data = pred_df,
    aes(x = time_id, ymin = lwr, ymax = upr),
    fill  = "#3498db", alpha = 0.12,
    inherit.aes = FALSE
  ) +

  # Regression line
  geom_line(
    data = pred_df,
    aes(x = time_id, y = fit),
    colour    = "#3498db",
    linetype  = "dashed",
    linewidth = 1,
    inherit.aes = FALSE
  ) +

  # Connecting line between points
  geom_line(colour = "grey60", linewidth = 0.7) +

  # Data points — coloured by resistance level
  geom_point(
    aes(fill = pct_r),
    shape = 21, size = 5,
    colour = "white", stroke = 1.2
  ) +

  # Colour scale: green → amber → red
  scale_fill_gradientn(
    colours = c("#27ae60", "#f39c12", "#e74c3c"),
    values  = rescale(c(0, 50, 100)),
    limits  = c(0, 100),
    name    = "Resistance (%)"
  ) +

  # Quarter labels on x-axis
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = 1:4,
    labels = levels(kp_trend$quarter)
  ) +

  scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(0, 100),
    labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%")
  ) +

  # Regression equation annotation
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = 1, y = 95,
    label = paste0(
      "β = +", slope, "% per quarter\n",
      "R² = ", r2, "\n",
      "p = ", p_value
    ),
    hjust  = 0, vjust = 1,
    size   = 3.5, colour = "#2c3e50",
    fontface = "italic"
  ) +

  labs(
    title    = "K. pneumoniae ciprofloxacin resistance — quarterly trend",
    subtitle = "Dashed line = linear regression | Shaded area = 95% confidence interval",
    x        = "Quarter",
    y        = "Resistance rate (%)",
    caption  = "Data source: Teaching dataset | First isolates only"
  )

p_scatter

Interpreting the trend plot

  • Beta (β): The slope of the regression line, in units of “% change per quarter.” A positive β (e.g., +3.2%) means resistance is rising each quarter.
  • R²: How well the time variable explains the variation in resistance. R² = 0.95 means 95% of the variation in resistance is explained by time — a strong trend.
  • p-value: Tests whether the slope is statistically significantly different from zero. p < 0.05 means the trend is unlikely to be due to chance alone.

Important limitation: With only 4 time points, the statistical power is low. Even a significant p-value should be interpreted cautiously with so few data points. Always note this limitation when reporting.

8.3 4.3 Correlation: Consumption vs Resistance

# ── Summarise ciprofloxacin consumption by quarter ────────────────────────
cip_consumption <- consumption %>%
  filter(str_detect(drug_name, "Cipro|cipro")) %>%
  group_by(quarter) %>%
  summarise(
    cip_ddd_1000pd = round(sum(ddd_dispensed) / sum(patient_days) * 1000, 1),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Join with resistance data
corr_df <- left_join(
  cip_consumption,
  quarterly_r %>% dplyr::select(quarter, kp_cip),
  by = "quarter"
) %>%
  filter(!is.na(kp_cip))

# Pearson correlation
cor_test <- cor.test(corr_df$cip_ddd_1000pd, corr_df$kp_cip, method = "pearson")
cat(sprintf("Pearson r = %.3f | p = %.4f\n",
            cor_test$estimate, cor_test$p.value))
## Pearson r = 0.861 | p = 0.1394
# ── Scatter: DDD vs resistance ────────────────────────────────────────────
p_corr <- ggplot(corr_df,
                 aes(x = cip_ddd_1000pd, y = kp_cip,
                     label = quarter)) +

  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE,
              colour = "grey60", fill = "grey90",
              linewidth = 0.8) +

  geom_point(aes(colour = quarter), size = 5, shape = 17) +

  geom_text(vjust = -1, hjust = 0.5, size = 3.5, fontface = "bold") +

  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c("Q1-2022" = "#3498db", "Q2-2022" = "#e67e22",
               "Q3-2022" = "#e74c3c", "Q4-2022" = "#27ae60"),
    guide = "none"
  ) +

  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(65, 95),
                     labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%")) +

  labs(
    title    = "Ciprofloxacin consumption vs K. pneumoniae resistance",
    subtitle = paste0("Pearson r = ", round(cor_test$estimate, 3),
                      " | Ecological correlation — not individual-level causation"),
    x        = "Ciprofloxacin DDD per 1,000 patient days",
    y        = "K. pneumoniae CIP resistance (%)",
    caption  = "Each point = one quarter | Regression line with 95% CI shown"
  )

p_corr
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
## Warning: The following aesthetics were dropped during statistical transformation: label.
## ℹ This can happen when ggplot fails to infer the correct grouping structure in
##   the data.
## ℹ Did you forget to specify a `group` aesthetic or to convert a numerical
##   variable into a factor?

The Ecological Fallacy — a critical warning

When we correlate ward-level antibiotic consumption with ward-level resistance rates, we are doing an ecological study. This means:

  • We cannot say that patients who received ciprofloxacin are more likely to become resistant
  • Many other factors (clonal outbreak, patient mix, infection control) drive resistance
  • The correlation may be spurious, especially with only 4 quarterly data points

Always add this caveat when reporting consumption-resistance correlations.

9 Visualization 5: Heatmap — The Full Resistance Overview

When to use a heatmap in AMR surveillance

A heatmap is the information-densest AMR visualization. It shows resistance rates for many organism-antibiotic combinations at once using colour intensity.

It is the standard format for: - Annual antibiogram presentations to infection control committees - Hospital-level AMR reports to management - Quick identification of which organism-antibiotic combinations are in crisis

9.1 Calculate all resistance rates

# ── Calculate resistance for all priority organism-antibiotic pairs ────────
# We build a helper function then call it for each pair

pairs <- list(
  list(org="Klebsiella pneumoniae",   ab="cip"), list(org="Klebsiella pneumoniae",   ab="mem"),
  list(org="Klebsiella pneumoniae",   ab="ctx"), list(org="Klebsiella pneumoniae",   ab="gen"),
  list(org="Escherichia coli",        ab="amp"), list(org="Escherichia coli",        ab="ctx"),
  list(org="Escherichia coli",        ab="cip"), list(org="Escherichia coli",        ab="sxt"),
  list(org="Staphylococcus aureus",   ab="oxa"), list(org="Staphylococcus aureus",   ab="gen"),
  list(org="Staphylococcus aureus",   ab="cip"),
  list(org="Pseudomonas aeruginosa",  ab="mem"), list(org="Pseudomonas aeruginosa",  ab="cip"),
  list(org="Acinetobacter baumannii", ab="mem"), list(org="Acinetobacter baumannii", ab="cip"),
  list(org="Acinetobacter baumannii", ab="col")
)

all_resist <- bind_rows(lapply(pairs, function(p) {
  calc_resistance_ci(clean_data, p$org, p$ab)
}))

# Short organism names
all_resist <- all_resist %>%
  mutate(
    org_short = case_when(
      organism == "Klebsiella pneumoniae"   ~ "K. pneumoniae",
      organism == "Escherichia coli"        ~ "E. coli",
      organism == "Staphylococcus aureus"   ~ "S. aureus",
      organism == "Pseudomonas aeruginosa"  ~ "P. aeruginosa",
      organism == "Acinetobacter baumannii" ~ "A. baumannii",
      TRUE ~ organism
    ),
    # Flag small sample sizes
    label = paste0(pct_R, "%", ifelse(n_tested < 30, "*", ""))
  )

# Preview
all_resist %>%
  dplyr::select(org_short, antibiotic, n_tested, pct_R, label) %>%
  kable(caption = "Table 4. Resistance rates for heatmap") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "striped")
Table 4. Resistance rates for heatmap
org_short antibiotic n_tested pct_R label
K. pneumoniae CIP 33 36.4 36.4%
K. pneumoniae MEM 33 33.3 33.3%
K. pneumoniae CTX 33 42.4 42.4%
K. pneumoniae GEN 33 36.4 36.4%
E. coli AMP 43 32.6 32.6%
E. coli CTX 43 30.2 30.2%
E. coli CIP 43 41.9 41.9%
E. coli SXT 43 25.6 25.6%
S. aureus OXA 22 36.4 36.4%*
S. aureus GEN 22 36.4 36.4%*
S. aureus CIP 22 31.8 31.8%*
P. aeruginosa MEM 11 27.3 27.3%*
P. aeruginosa CIP 11 54.5 54.5%*
A. baumannii MEM 17 52.9 52.9%*
A. baumannii CIP 17 52.9 52.9%*
A. baumannii COL 17 29.4 29.4%*

9.2 Create the heatmap

# ── Order organisms by clinical priority ──────────────────────────────────
org_order <- c("A. baumannii","P. aeruginosa","K. pneumoniae",
                "E. coli","S. aureus")
ab_order  <- c("COL","MEM","CTX","AMP","CIP","GEN","SXT","OXA")

all_resist <- all_resist %>%
  mutate(
    org_short  = factor(org_short,  levels = org_order),
    antibiotic = factor(antibiotic, levels = ab_order)
  )

p_heat <- ggplot(
  all_resist,
  aes(x = antibiotic, y = org_short, fill = pct_R)
) +

  # Tiles — white lines between tiles act as grid lines
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 1.5) +

  # Text label inside each tile
  geom_text(
    aes(label = label,
        colour = pct_R > 50),   # white text on dark tiles, dark on light
    size      = 3.5,
    fontface  = "bold"
  ) +

  # Colour scale: green → amber → red
  scale_fill_gradientn(
    colours   = c("#27ae60", "#f1c40f", "#e74c3c"),
    values    = rescale(c(0, 40, 100)),
    limits    = c(0, 100),
    na.value  = "grey85",
    name      = "Resistance (%)",
    guide     = guide_colourbar(barwidth = 12, barheight = 0.5,
                                title.position = "top",
                                title.hjust    = 0.5)
  ) +

  # Text colour: white on high-resistance tiles, dark on low
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("TRUE" = "white", "FALSE" = "#2c3e50"),
                      guide = "none") +

  labs(
    title    = "Antimicrobial resistance heatmap — 2022",
    subtitle = "Priority pathogens × Antibiotics | *n < 30 isolates (interpret with caution) | Grey = not tested",
    x        = "Antibiotic",
    y        = NULL,
    caption  = "Data source: Teaching dataset | First isolates only | EUCAST 2025 breakpoints"
  ) +

  theme(
    axis.text.y     = element_text(face = "italic", size = 11),
    axis.text.x     = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    panel.grid      = element_blank()
  )

p_heat

Reading the heatmap

  • Dark red tiles = high resistance — these organism-antibiotic combinations are failing and cannot be used empirically
  • Green tiles = low resistance — these remain effective
  • Grey tiles = not tested (e.g., colistin not tested for S. aureus because Gram-positives are intrinsically resistant)
  • **Asterisk (*)** = fewer than 30 isolates — the rate is unreliable

Pattern to look for: If an entire row is red (like A. baumannii), that organism is approaching XDR (extensively drug resistant) status and management requires specialist input.

10 Combining Charts: Creating a Dashboard Figure

For reports and presentations, you often want to show multiple related charts together. The patchwork package makes this easy:

# ── Combine the bar plot and heatmap into one figure ──────────────────────
combined <- (p_bar / p_heat) +
  plot_annotation(
    title   = "AMR Surveillance Dashboard — Kpandai District Hospital, 2022",
    caption = "First isolates only | EUCAST 2025 breakpoints | *n < 30",
    theme   = theme(
      plot.title   = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold",
                                   colour = "#1A4A8A", hjust = 0.5),
      plot.caption = element_text(size = 9, colour = "grey50")
    )
  ) +
  plot_layout(heights = c(1.2, 1))

combined

# ── Save dashboard figure ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
ggsave(
  "AMR_Dashboard_2022.png",
  plot   = combined,
  width  = 13,
  height = 10,
  dpi    = 300
)

11 Putting It All Together: A Complete Reporting Workflow

# ── COMPLETE WORKFLOW — COPY THIS FOR YOUR OWN HOSPITAL DATA ──────────────

# 1. Set your file path
my_file <- "path/to/your/data.xlsx"

# 2. Import
raw <- read_excel(my_file, sheet = "YourSheetName") %>% clean_names()

# 3. Clean
clean <- raw %>%
  filter(!is.na(patient_id)) %>%                          # remove blanks
  mutate(organism_std = str_to_title(str_trim(organism))) # standardise names

# 4. First isolate rule
analysis <- clean %>%
  arrange(patient_id, organism_std, collection_date) %>%
  group_by(patient_id, organism_std) %>%
  slice(1) %>%  # keep only the first row per group
  ungroup()

# 5. Calculate resistance
results <- calc_resistance_ci(analysis, "Klebsiella pneumoniae", "cip")

# 6. Plot
p <- ggplot(results, aes(x = antibiotic, y = pct_R, fill = pct_R)) +
  geom_col() + amr_theme

# 7. Save
ggsave("my_resistance_plot.png", plot = p, width = 8, height = 5, dpi = 300)

12 Session Information

It is good practice to include session information at the end of your R Markdown document. This records exactly which versions of R and each package were used — important for reproducibility.

sessionInfo()
## R version 4.5.1 (2025-06-13)
## Platform: aarch64-apple-darwin20
## Running under: macOS Tahoe 26.2
## 
## Matrix products: default
## BLAS:   /Library/Frameworks/R.framework/Versions/4.5-arm64/Resources/lib/libRblas.0.dylib 
## LAPACK: /Library/Frameworks/R.framework/Versions/4.5-arm64/Resources/lib/libRlapack.dylib;  LAPACK version 3.12.1
## 
## locale:
## [1] en_US.UTF-8/en_US.UTF-8/en_US.UTF-8/C/en_US.UTF-8/en_US.UTF-8
## 
## time zone: Africa/Accra

Next steps

  1. Exercise : Use this Rmd with your own hospital’s WHONET export. Follow the same cleaning steps.

  2. Explore the AMR package in R — purpose-built for clinical microbiology data analysis with built-in EUCAST/CLSI breakpoints.
